source.title: Sketchy fit notes and probing queries — Oliver Lin
source.content:
Quick sketch: Oliver Lin — runtime SWE candidate (loop 2)

Context / meta:
- Interviewer: Jordan Blake (notes here), loop included 1x system-design, 1x coding, 1x behavioral + team match.
- Role we’re hiring for: Senior Runtime Engineer (inference-focused) — emphasis: batching, kv cache, graceful degradation.
- Timeboxed: 60m system design, 40m algo/code. Candidate comfortable with C++/Rust and some Go.

High-level impression (gut):
- Smart, pragmatic. Good instinct for production tradeoffs (latency vs cost).
- Less polished on low-level memory accounting and some concurrency corner cases.
- Great at framing tradeoffs and owner mentality — would be a good match for a product-forward runtime team if paired with a strong SRE for correctness/borrowing discipline.

Signals — strengths:
- Prefix/KV cache intuition: gave clear examples of when to do per-request vs shared caches, how to invalidate on model version change. Mentioned bloom filters to avoid hot-miss amplification (nice practical heuristic).
- Batching strategy: argues for adaptive batching by request latency budget with percentile-aware thresholds; sketched simple PID-like controller to tune batch formation. Shows real-world thinking.
- Observability: asked about token-level tracing, cost attribution (per-route token breakdown). Suggested lightweight sampling for long requests.
- Ownership: concrete story about owning a degradation-fallback flow at previous company — rolled out A/B and had rollback triggers based on latency P95.

Signals — concerns / weak spots:
- Concurrency primitives: when pressed on lock-free kv cache design, answer surfaced usual ideas (sharded locks, RCU) but lacked specifics on ABA and memory reclamation. Could be surface-level familiarity rather than deep systems engineering.
- Memory accounting at scale: didn’t show a robust approach to prevent cache OOM under adversarial traffic. Talked about LRU + soft quotas but not about admission policies or token-based budgeting.
- Quantization + kernel tradeoffs: knows the general landscape but not microbenching details (didn’t cite how quantization impacted KV layout or cache locality).
- Code exercise: passed but used higher-level libraries; implementation had edge-case bugs (off-by-one in ring buffer), fixed after hint. Good problem-solving but needs careful review in production code.

Representative quotes / verbatim: (to capture tone)
- “I prefer adaptive batching because fixed windows either waste tokens or blow latency targets.”
- “If model version changes, we should invalidate the cache and warm back with prioritised cold-start requests.”

Open questions to resolve (follow-ups):
- Specific design for memory-safe lock-free KV cache: ask for a follow-up sketch that shows how they would handle reclamation (hazard pointers/epoch) and how they'd test it under churn.
- Failure modes: ask candidate to enumerate failure modes for a shared KV cache across multiple tenants (stale entries, eviction storms, memory hogging, cross-tenant poisoning) and specific mitigations.
- Benchmarks: ask for a short plan on how they would benchmark different quantization/attention kernels and what signals they'd look for (cache misses, vectorized instructions per cycle, memory bandwidth saturation).

Recommended next steps / small homework (low-friction):
- 2-hour whiteboard + writeup: design a small in-memory KV cache for LLM KV stores that supports eviction, versioned invalidation, and per-tenant quotas. Include concurrency plan and test strategy.
- Optional micro-bench: give them a tiny harness to measure read latency under frenemy traffic (~10k qps) and ask for a 1-page writeup of findings.

Risk/fit assessment (short rubric):
- Technical ownership: 4/5 (strong product/ops stories)
- Low-level systems rigor: 3/5 (needs deeper concurrency/memory-freedom experience)
- Team fit / communication: 5/5 (clear, collaborative, good at explaining tradeoffs)
- Overall: lean-hire with follow-up to confirm systems depth; good match if paired with SRE/bench lead on onboarding.

Suggested hiring recommendation language (for panel):
- "Strengths: pragmatic production engineer, strong batching and ownership instincts, likely to drive quick wins on throughput and cost."
- "Concerns: some gaps in low-level concurrent memory management; recommend a focused follow-up problem to validate."

Concrete probing questions for follow-up (quick list to copy into loop invite):
1) Draw and explain a lock-free KV cache with multiple readers/writers: how do you reclaim nodes safely?
2) Describe an admission policy to prevent eviction storms during a traffic spike — how do you prioritize entries?
3) Suppose we see a P95 latency regression after a quantization rollout; what signals do you pull, and how do you narrow root cause (kernel, attention pattern, memory thrash)?
4) How would you test an adaptive batching controller offline and in prod to avoid oscillations?

Notes-to-self (action items):
- Send candidate the whiteboard prompt and set a 48h window for return.
- Loop hosts: Aisha to own system-design follow-up; Diego to own benching ask.
- If follow-ups look good, arrange final culture/manager sync within a week.

Misc / kitchen-sink ideas (rough):
- If hire: early work could be improving warm-start heuristics (priority warm-up for cache misses), and a tight observability dashboard showing cache hit by prompt prefix.
- Consider pairing him with someone experienced in memory reclamation for first 3 months (mentorship plan).

Unresolved flags:
- Need clearer evidence of handling pathological concurrency under adversarial input.
- Verify depth of quantization microbenchmarking (ask for past benchmark artifacts if available).

End of notes.

source.title: autoscale-policy-simulator-results-and-isolation-notes
source.content:
Purpose:
This is a working doc capturing results from the autoscale policy simulator runs we executed after the Feb 26 dedicated pool degradation, plus a draft isolation checklist for noisy-neighbor-like behavior observed during the incident. Goal: produce a compact runbook patch we can ship into on-call playbooks and iterate from there.

Context / brief incident summary (Feb 26 chain):
- 2026-02-26 03:17 UTC: first alert — elevated GPU queuing latency on dedicated-gpu-pool-3; tracer showed continuous token service latencies rising for 30+ mins.
- 03:22 UTC: autoscaler attempted scale-up but did not acquire spare nodes due to mis-evaluated capacity (policy threshold incorrectly used pod_cpu_request instead of pod_gpu_request).
- 03:40 UTC: noisy tenant (svc-internal-batch) started heavy memory allocations causing localized OOM + driver resets on 2/16 GPUs.
- 04:05 UTC: manual eviction of offending pods cleared queue; latency recovered after 6 minutes.
- Root causes (preliminary): autoscale policy mismatch + lack of high-fidelity isolation metric (GPU memory pressure not surfaced to policies).

What I ran: autoscale-policy simulator (local harness)
- Dataset: historical production traces from 2026-02-01 to 2026-02-28 (token request rates + replica counts + per-tenant memory-usage samples).
- Variants: current policy (v1), proposed policy (v2: use gpu_memory_request + burst headroom), aggressive policy (v3: scale faster but with cooldown), conservative (v4: larger cooldown).
- Failure injection: sim injected noisy-tenant spike (x10 mem allocation) and driver-reset events at t+30m.
- Metrics tracked: time-to-scale, max-queued-requests, percent-requests-breaching-latency-SLO, cost-estimate delta.

High-level simulator findings:
- v1 (current): reproduced queuing and failure to scale in 72% of runs where noisy-tenant spike coincided with a partial node shortage. Median time-to-recovery 18m.
- v2 (gpu_memory_request): 85% reduction in failure-to-scale cases. Time-to-scale reduced from 12m -> 4m median. Cost delta: +3.1% on our trace set.
- v3 (aggressive): nearly zero simulated outages but cost +9%. High risk of oscillation without better cooldown logic.
- v4 (conservative): fewer spurious scale events but still vulnerable to fast memory-pressure attacks.
- Additional note: driver-reset injections cause stateful allocations to leak; autoscaler needs to be paired with eviction thresholds and an explicit eviction blacklist for problematic tenants.

Telemetry correlations observed during runs (what to add to SLO dashboards):
- Per-VM GPU memory-utilization time-series (5s resolution) is the most predictive signal for queuing.
- Driver-reset rate (dmesg/gpu-reset counter) spikes precede latency spikes by ~30s.
- Per-tenant persistent kernel allocation (tracked via cgroup annotations) correlates strongly with post-reset degraded throughput.

Immediate mitigation playbook (short steps for on-call):
1) Identify affected pool: `rbctl pools list --filter=dedicated-gpu-pool-3` (confirm queuing metrics > 95th percentile).
2) Check GPU memory pressure: `rbctl metrics query "gpu_memory_used" --pool=dedicated-gpu-pool-3 --window=10m`.
3) If memory pressure high and driver-reset rate > 0: mark suspected tenants via `rbctl tenant top --pool=... --by=gpu_memory` and push `rbctl tenant annotate <tenant> noisy-candidate`.
4) Evict offending pods with graceful drain: `rbctl tenant evict --tenant=<id> --grace=30s --reason="noisy-neighbor-isolation"`.
5) If autoscaler failed to request nodes: trigger manual scale-up `rbctl pool resize --pool=dedicated-gpu-pool-3 --add-nodes=2 --reason=oncall-manual-scale`.
6) If manual scale-up is blocked (quota/limit): fallback to routing policy to hosted / lower-tier models: `rbctl route set-fallback --pool=dedicated-gpu-pool-3 --fallback=hosted`.
7) Post-recovery: collect full traces (`rbctl trace export --range=...`) and attach to ticket JIRA-9876.

Draft runbook additions (to push into docs/ops/playbooks):
- New autoscaler input metric: add gpu_memory_request percentiles (p50/p90/p99) as first-class scaling key.
- Add driver-reset counter as an early-warning signal; tie to pre-emptive tenant throttles.
- Create an "isolate-noisy-tenant" short play with CLI examples (see Immediate mitigation steps).
- Add a documented cooldown policy: require 3 successive scale-down health-check windows before decrementing replica count to avoid oscillation when using aggressive scale-up.

Action items (owners + naive deadlines):
- Add gpu_memory_request to autoscaler policy and run expanded sims (owner: Priya Desai, due: 2026-03-05).
- Implement driver-reset metric export in metrics pipeline (owner: Miguel Torres, due: 2026-03-12).
- Ship on-call CLI short-circuit commands into `rbctl` (owner: Samira Khan, due: 2026-02-28). Status: partial (evict and resize commands exist; annotate/tenant top in progress).
- Create tenant-scoped eviction blacklist and add svc-internal-batch as temporary measure (owner: Jordan Blake, due: 2026-02-28). Status: done.

Open questions / discussion items:
- How do we define "noisy" vs. "legitimate heavy" workload? Proposal: require two signals (gpu_memory_pressure AND disproportionate queue growth compared to request-rate).
- Can we have a graceful automatic throttle instead of eviction? Throttling needs a tenant-level token-bucket aware of GPU memory usage.
- Is there a legal / customer impact consideration for evicting tenant workloads that have paid reserved capacity? Need product policy guidance.

Quick retrospective notes from last incident that aren't captured in telemetry:
- Oncall confusion: the autoscaler alerts were phrased "node deficit" but lack a link to the exact policy that fired — make alerts linkable to code/line in policy config.
- SRE took longer to decide to evict because the tooling did not surface tenants sorted by GPU memory usage. Fix is high-priority (see action items).

References and artifacts to attach to final postmortem:
- JIRA-9876 (primary incident ticket), Confluence sketch at /pages/INC-2026-01-autoscale-sim, PR-214 (ops change to `rbctl`), simulation logs (s3://redwood-sims/2026-02-autoscale-run-7.tar.gz).

Next steps for this doc:
- Convert immediate mitigation section into a trimmed playbook (2–3 commands) and publish to oncall handbook (owner: Samira Khan).
- Priya to run the expanded sim matrix (v2+v3 combos) against March org load and report cost/reliability tradeoffs in a follow-up doc.
- Revisit tenant eviction policy once driver-reset metric is live.

Notes on uniqueness / useful heuristics for future incidents:
- Treat GPU memory pressure as a first-class resource for autoscaling decisions, not an afterthought.
- Prefer slow scale-down cooldowns to aggressive scale-up; this avoids oscillation when paired with bursty noisy tenants.
- Eviction should be last resort for reserved tenants; automatic throttles are preferable for multi-tenant fairness.

Unresolved bits to document in full postmortem: exact sequence of grpc retries during the 03:22-03:40 window and whether a single orchestration controller instance had a transient partition.

Contact / oncall follow-ups:
- Primary responder: Jordan Blake (@jblake)
- Escalation: Priya Desai (@pdesai)
- Metrics lead: Miguel Torres (@mtorres)

-- End of working notes --

(If you edit this doc, please keep headings and add your initials + date for quick traceability.)

text:
Purpose:
This is a working doc capturing results from the autoscale policy simulator runs we executed after the Feb 26 dedicated pool degradation, plus a draft isolation checklist for noisy-neighbor-like behavior observed during the incident. Goal: produce a compact runbook patch we can ship into on-call playbooks and iterate from there.

Context / brief incident summary (Feb 26 chain):
- 2026-02-26 03:17 UTC: first alert — elevated GPU queuing latency on dedicated-gpu-pool-3; tracer showed continuous token service latencies rising for 30+ mins.
- 03:22 UTC: autoscaler attempted scale-up but did not acquire spare nodes due to mis-evaluated capacity (policy threshold incorrectly used pod_cpu_request instead of pod_gpu_request).
- 03:40 UTC: noisy tenant (svc-internal-batch) started heavy memory allocations causing localized OOM + driver resets on 2/16 GPUs.
- 04:05 UTC: manual eviction of offending pods cleared queue; latency recovered after 6 minutes.
- Root causes (preliminary): autoscale policy mismatch + lack of high-fidelity isolation metric (GPU memory pressure not surfaced to policies).

What I ran: autoscale-policy simulator (local harness)
- Dataset: historical production traces from 2026-02-01 to 2026-02-28 (token request rates + replica counts + per-tenant memory-usage samples).
- Variants: current policy (v1), proposed policy (v2: use gpu_memory_request + burst headroom), aggressive policy (v3: scale faster but with cooldown), conservative (v4: larger cooldown).
- Failure injection: sim injected noisy-tenant spike (x10 mem allocation) and driver-reset events at t+30m.
- Metrics tracked: time-to-scale, max-queued-requests, percent-requests-breaching-latency-SLO, cost-estimate delta.

High-level simulator findings:
- v1 (current): reproduced queuing and failure to scale in 72% of runs where noisy-tenant spike coincided with a partial node shortage. Median time-to-recovery 18m.
- v2 (gpu_memory_request): 85% reduction in failure-to-scale cases. Time-to-scale reduced from 12m -> 4m median. Cost delta: +3.1% on our trace set.
- v3 (aggressive): nearly zero simulated outages but cost +9%. High risk of oscillation without better cooldown logic.
- v4 (conservative): fewer spurious scale events but still vulnerable to fast memory-pressure attacks.
- Additional note: driver-reset injections cause stateful allocations to leak; autoscaler needs to be paired with eviction thresholds and an explicit eviction blacklist for problematic tenants.

Telemetry correlations observed during runs (what to add to SLO dashboards):
- Per-VM GPU memory-utilization time-series (5s resolution) is the most predictive signal for queuing.
- Driver-reset rate (dmesg/gpu-reset counter) spikes precede latency spikes by ~30s.
- Per-tenant persistent kernel allocation (tracked via cgroup annotations) correlates strongly with post-reset degraded throughput.

Immediate mitigation playbook (short steps for on-call):
1) Identify affected pool: `rbctl pools list --filter=dedicated-gpu-pool-3` (confirm queuing metrics > 95th percentile).
2) Check GPU memory pressure: `rbctl metrics query \"gpu_memory_used\" --pool=dedicated-gpu-pool-3 --window=10m`.
3) If memory pressure high and driver-reset rate > 0: mark suspected tenants via `rbctl tenant top --pool=... --by=gpu_memory` and push `rbctl tenant annotate <tenant> noisy-candidate`.
4) Evict offending pods with graceful drain: `rbctl tenant evict --tenant=<id> --grace=30s --reason=\"noisy-neighbor-isolation\"`.
5) If autoscaler failed to request nodes: trigger manual scale-up `rbctl pool resize --pool=dedicated-gpu-pool-3 --add-nodes=2 --reason=oncall-manual-scale`.
6) If manual scale-up is blocked (quota/limit): fallback to routing policy to hosted / lower-tier models: `rbctl route set-fallback --pool=dedicated-gpu-pool-3 --fallback=hosted`.
7) Post-recovery: collect full traces (`rbctl trace export --range=...`) and attach to ticket JIRA-9876.

Draft runbook additions (to push into docs/ops/playbooks):
- New autoscaler input metric: add gpu_memory_request percentiles (p50/p90/p99) as first-class scaling key.
- Add driver-reset counter as an early-warning signal; tie to pre-emptive tenant throttles.
- Create an \"isolate-noisy-tenant\" short play with CLI examples (see Immediate mitigation steps).
- Add a documented cooldown policy: require 3 successive scale-down health-check windows before decrementing replica count to avoid oscillation when using aggressive scale-up.

Action items (owners + naive deadlines):
- Add gpu_memory_request to autoscaler policy and run expanded sims (owner: Priya Desai, due: 2026-03-05).
- Implement driver-reset metric export in metrics pipeline (owner: Miguel Torres, due: 2026-03-12).
- Ship on-call CLI short-circuit commands into `rbctl` (owner: Samira Khan, due: 2026-02-28). Status: partial (evict and resize commands exist; annotate/tenant top in progress).
- Create tenant-scoped eviction blacklist and add svc-internal-batch as temporary measure (owner: Jordan Blake, due: 2026-02-28). Status: done.

Open questions / discussion items:
- How do we define \"noisy\" vs. \"legitimate heavy\" workload? Proposal: require two signals (gpu_memory_pressure AND disproportionate queue growth compared to request-rate).
- Can we have a graceful automatic throttle instead of eviction? Throttling needs a tenant-level token-bucket aware of GPU memory usage.
- Is there a legal / customer impact consideration for evicting tenant workloads that have paid reserved capacity? Need product policy guidance.

Quick retrospective notes from last incident that aren't captured in telemetry:
- Oncall confusion: the autoscaler alerts were phrased "node deficit" but lack a link to the exact policy that fired — make alerts linkable to code/line in policy config.
- SRE took longer to decide to evict because the tooling did not surface tenants sorted by GPU memory usage. Fix is high-priority (see action items).

References and artifacts to attach to final postmortem:
- JIRA-9876 (primary incident ticket), Confluence sketch at /pages/INC-2026-01-autoscale-sim, PR-214 (ops change to `rbctl`), simulation logs (s3://redwood-sims/2026-02-autoscale-run-7.tar.gz).

Next steps for this doc:
- Convert immediate mitigation section into a trimmed playbook (2–3 commands) and publish to oncall handbook (owner: Samira Khan).
- Priya to run the expanded sim matrix (v2+v3 combos) against March org load and report cost/reliability tradeoffs in a follow-up doc.
- Revisit tenant eviction policy once driver-reset metric is live.

Notes on uniqueness / useful heuristics for future incidents:
- Treat GPU memory pressure as a first-class resource for autoscaling decisions, not an afterthought.
- Prefer slow scale-down cooldowns to aggressive scale-up; this avoids oscillation when paired with bursty noisy tenants.
- Eviction should be last resort for reserved tenants; automatic throttles are preferable for multi-tenant fairness.

Unresolved bits to document in full postmortem: exact sequence of grpc retries during the 03:22-03:40 window and whether a single orchestration controller instance had a transient partition.

Contact / oncall follow-ups:
- Primary responder: Jordan Blake (@jblake)
- Escalation: Priya Desai (@pdesai)
- Metrics lead: Miguel Torres (@mtorres)

-- End of working notes --

source.title: auth rate sleuth logbook
source.content:
Context:
- Sporadic 401s and 429s observed from mobile app (iOS release channel) calling /v1/generate and /v1/chat/completions between 2026-03-02 and 2026-03-09. Desktop and server-to-server traffic largely unaffected.
- Users report transient failures ~1-3s into a session or immediately after app background/foreground.
- Hypothesis: short-lived client key + network retries causing quota bursts, or a bug in auth cache eviction.

Raw traces / snippets (redacted keys and ids):
- Example curl that reproduces locally (simulated):
  curl -v https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate 
    -H "Authorization: Bearer <REDACTED_KEY>" 
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" 
    -d '{"model":"redwood-small","input":"say hi"}'

- 401 response body seen in mobile logs:
  2026-03-05T18:21:12.482Z request_id=3a9f8b2a status=401 path=/v1/generate src=mobile-ios-1
  response: {"error":"invalid_api_key","message":"API key not found or revoked"}

- 429 response pattern from edge logs (same timeframe):
  2026-03-05T18:21:12.486Z request_id=3a9f8b2b status=429 path=/v1/generate src=edge-us-west-2
  headers: x-rate-limit-limit: 120, x-rate-limit-remaining: 0, x-rate-limit-reset: 1678047670, retry-after: 1
  response: {"error":"rate_limited","message":"Token burst"}

- Interesting timeline for a single session (mobile app):
  - t=0 app resumes from background, sets Authorization header with ephemeral client key (issued by mobile auth service)
  - t=0.2 first request -> 401 invalid_api_key
  - t=0.7 second request -> 200 OK
  - t=1.2 third request -> 429 x-rate-limit-remaining=0

Commands I ran (experiments):
- Replayed client flow from emulator, adding 100ms gap between requests: reproduced 401 then 429 sequence.
- Issued the same key from curl repeatedly with 200 concurrent connections (wrk) -> immediate 429s and tokens get blacklisted in edge-cache for ~30s.
- Rotated client API key with extremely short TTL (30s) -> observed auth backend sometimes returning 200 while edge still had old key cached, causing mismatch and 401s.
- Disabled client-side retry backoff and retried manually -> reduced 429 counts by ~40% in synthetic tests.

Logs / headers to look for in production:
- auth-service logs for key issuance and revocation around request timestamps (correlate issuance_id).
- edge cache eviction logs: keys removed or flagged as stale.
- per-token counters in quota store (redis/hll) for spikes.
- request_id correlation between 401 and subsequent 429 (same session).

Working hypotheses (ranked):
1) Token TTL + client retries combine to create temporary auth mismatch: mobile obtains a new key A, edge still cached old key B -> first request 401 (auth backend sees new key ok but edge returns 401?) Need to confirm which component returns 401.
2) Clients doing aggressive retry (no exponential backoff) create short token burst per-device that trips per-key rate-limit thresholds (especially when keys are shared per-account across sessions).
3) Edge key-validation cache bug: when key B is evicted incorrectly, edge rejects while auth-service accepts -> 401s even though auth backend would accept if bypassed.
4) Misapplied global quota enforcement: certain routes (/v1/generate) counted twice (edge + gateway) erroneously doubling rate accounting leading to premature 429.

Evidence supporting hypotheses:
- Evidence for #2: Disabling retries cuts synthetic 429 rate ~40%.
- Evidence for #1/#3: Timeline shows 401 then 200 then 429 within seconds; implies state inconsistency between components.
- Evidence for #4: Edge metrics show ~2x counting on some routes in the same timeframe (need to verify in prometheus queries).

Open questions / next tests:
- Which service returns 401? Edge/gateway or auth-service? Add explicit logging: "%component% returned 401 for token=%token_hash%".
- Add sampling that logs full header chain and token fingerprint (sha256 of key) to correlate across layers.
- Try issuing long-lived test key and replay same mobile sequence from device to see if 401 disappears.
- Temporarily enable strict retry-backoff on mobile staging to measure reduction.
- Run a controlled experiment toggling double-counting check in edge metrics to confirm hypothesis #4.

Quick reproduction scripts (for me):
- python requests sketch:
  import requests, time
  headers = {"Authorization":"Bearer <TEST_KEY>"}
  for i in range(10):
      r = requests.post("https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate", json={"model":"redwood-small","input":"x"}, headers=headers)
      print(i, r.status_code, r.headers.get('x-rate-limit-remaining'), r.text[:120])
      time.sleep(0.15)

- Node fetch with rapid retry (to simulate mobile):
  for (let i=0;i<12;i++) fetch(url,{headers}).then(r=>r.text().then(t=>console.log(i,r.status))).catch(e=>console.log('err',e));

Safety notes / things to avoid:
- Do NOT paste full API keys or JWTs into logs. Use sha256(key) or token fingerprint.
- When running high concurrency tests, coordinate with infra (avoid accidental DoS on prod edge).

Immediate action items (my todo):
- [ ] Add debug logs in edge for auth failure origin (who returned 401) — PR opened: GITHUB-PR inference/edge#782 (waiting review).
- [ ] Run controlled staging test with long-lived key (assigned to matt-r test account) — scheduled 2026-03-11.
- [ ] Patch mobile staging client to exponential backoff and test.
- [ ] Ask SRE to surface per-token counters in prometheus + trace retention for 30s windows.

Notes for handoff to oncall / SRE:
- Correlation keys: use request_id prefix 3a9f8b2* between 2026-03-05T18:20 and 18:25 UTC.
- Symptoms: 401 followed by 429 from the same session. Likely auth cache / retry amplification.
- If asked to mitigate quickly: temporarily increase per-key rate limit threshold for mobile account family or enable stricter client-side backoff via feature flag.

Misc observations / random thoughts:
- Mobile app uses a per-install ephemeral key minted by a small auth service. If that service skews TTL lower than edge cache TTL, we get stale state. Team should align TTLs or add a cache-invalidation hook when keys are rotated.
- Could consider adding a short-grace window where both old and new keys are valid for N seconds after rotation to avoid immediate 401s.

End log.

-- Jordan B.

source.title: KV prefetch throughput breadcrumbs
source.body:
Notes to self - ad hoc breadcrumbs for the throughput regression that popped up after the prefetch tuning work. Mostly raw commands, error snippets, and hypotheses. Keep this as an index for a more formal postmortem later.

Summary (quick):
- Regression observed 2025-11-05 after changing continuous prefetch to larger window to improve latency p50 for short requests. Instead saw sustained throughput drop under mixed sequence lengths during peak traffic.
- Symptoms: requests per second dropped ~3.5x under typical traffic profile; GPU utilization appeared high but token output stalled (low tokens/sec).
- Initial suspicion: prefetch window + batching interaction -> head-of-line blocking & KV cache thrash. Also possible: eager KV loads saturate PCIe/CPU memory copy path causing stalls.

Timeline / quick log of experiments:
- 2025-11-05 09:20 local reproduce: ramped synthetic load with seq_len mix [8, 512, 1024] using my ramp script. Observed throughput drop at ~t=150s.
- 2025-11-05 11:10 swapped config: continuous_batching.prefetch_window_ms from 1200 -> 80 (temp). Throughput returned to expected levels within 30s.
- 2025-11-06 14:45 re-ran with profiling: enabled TRACE logs on batcher + nvprof counters. Saw repeated warnings: 'WARN batcher: kv-prefetch: evicting-shard id=3 because active_prefetches=6 > max_prefetches=4'. Also 'WARN kernel: stall on memcpy (host->device) 300ms'.
- 2025-11-07 10:02 A/B test in canary: prefetch=1200 in canary, prefetch=80 in baseline. Canary p95 latency slightly better for short tails but throughput worse under mixed loads.

Commands tried (local cluster / devbox):
- Ramp load script (python):
  $ python tools/ramp_traffic.py --endpoint 'http://localhost:8080/v1/generate' --model 'rw1a' --mix 'short:60,medium:30,long:10' --rps 1200 --duration 600

- Quick curl for single shot to exercise KV path:
  $ curl -s -X POST 'http://localhost:8080/internal/exec' -d '{"op":"prefetch","shard":3,"seq_len":1024}' | jq .

- Enable TRACE logs for batcher & KV manager:
  $ docker exec -it runtime-app sh -c "REDWOOD_LOG=trace ./bin/runtime --log-target=batcher,kv_manager"

- Dump batcher state via /internal/debug endpoint:
  $ curl 'http://localhost:8080/internal/debug/batcher' > /tmp/batcher_dump.json
  (fields I looked at: queued_count, active_batches, prefetch_queue_len, max_prefetch_inflight)

Observed log snippets (copied, slightly sanitized):
- "WARN batcher: prefetch window expanded to 1200ms, inflight_prefetches=6, max_prefetches=4 -> throttling"
- "ERROR kv_manager: stale-kv-shard 3 access while loading new prefetch -> dropping request to avoid deadlock"
- "WARN gpu_copy: memcpy_h2d blocked 312ms (busy=98%), queue_depth=8"
- "INFO runtime: output_tokens=0 for 3 consecutive polls for request_id=abcd-1234, marking as delayed"

Metrics snapshot (before vs after tuning):
- Baseline (prefetch=80ms): avg tokens/sec = 18.5k, p95 latency = 220ms, GPU util = 68%
- Post-change (prefetch=1200ms): avg tokens/sec = 5.3k, p95 latency = 260ms (for short), GPU util = 92% but low token emission rate
- Notes: GPU util high but the per-GPU SM occupancy time is dominated by memcpy stalls and small kernel launches, so high util is misleading.

Hypotheses (ranked):
1) Large prefetch window leads to many in-flight prefetches for long sequences -> memory pressure on KV cache -> frequent evictions thrash and cause more prefetches -> cascade -> throughput collapse. Evidence: evict logs, memory warnings.
2) Prefetch scheduling interacts poorly with continuous batching: prefetch tasks serialized with token-generation tasks on the same GPU streams, causing blocking copies. Evidence: memcpy_h2d blocked logs and kernel scheduling patterns in nvprof trace.
3) Head-of-line blocking: large prefetch holds a request in the batcher longer while waiting prefetch completion; newer short requests queue behind and miss batching benefits. Evidence: p95 for short slightly improved in isolated tests but tail during mixed load spiked.
4) Configuration bug: max_prefetch_inflight not enforced correctly when dynamic shard rebalancing happens. Saw active_prefetches > configured max in logs. Could be race in atomic counters.

Quick repro notes (how I reproduce locally reliably):
- Use ramp_traffic with mix short:60, medium:30, long:10, rps 900-1200. Prefetch 1200ms reproduces < 30% baseline throughput in ~2 minutes.
- Capture batcher dump every 10s and nvprof for 60s starting at t=120.

Mitigations tried (and results):
- Lower prefetch_window_ms to 80: immediate recovery to baseline tokens/sec. (Short-term fix).
- Reduce max_prefetch_inflight from 8 -> 4: mitigated but throughput still noisy under bursts.
- Change kv_cache_policy from 'shared' to 'per-shard' (dev-only): reduced eviction chatter in logs but didn't fully restore throughput.
- Offload prefetch memcpy to separate cuda stream (experimental patch, PR#456): reduced memcpy stalls in trace, improved tokens/sec by ~20% in local test but not full recovery.

Open actions / TODOs:
- [ ] Reproduce on dedicated staging cluster (multi-node) with realistic ingress patterns (customer traces) - scheduled 2026-02-22.
- [ ] Add telemetry for 'prefetch_lifecycle' events: queued_ts, started_ts, finished_ts, bytes_copied, eviction_count. Need to link to request_id. (I sketched a patch, see PR notes).
- [ ] Audit atomic counters around active_prefetches / max_prefetch enforcement. Suspect race during shard reassign.
- [ ] Stress-test with artificially slowed H2D path to verify IO-bottleneck hypothesis.
- [ ] Postmortem doc (formal) once we have staging repro and regression test; include recommended default prefetch window and safe guard knobs.

Random notes / small ideas:
- Could add a small adaptive controller: if GPU memcpy stall fraction > X% then reduce prefetch window automatically. Keep heuristic conservative.
- Consider separating prefetch copy path to a dedicated DMA engine or use asynchronous pinned-memory pool to reduce blocking.
- Add 'prefetch priority' so short requests get higher priority for batch assembly even if a prefetch for a long request is inflight. Prevents short-request head-of-line.

Scratch commands / useful snippets for next time:
- dump batcher state repeatedly (every 5s):
  $ for i in `seq 1 40`; do curl 'http://localhost:8080/internal/debug/batcher' | jq .queued_count, .prefetch_queue_len, .active_prefetches >> /tmp/batcher_trace.txt; sleep 5; done

- capture nvprof (or Nsight) around the failure window:
  $ nsys profile -o profile --capture-range c -- cpulimit -- ./bin/runtime --config ...

- quick check for atomic enforcement bug (dev build):
  $ grep -n "active_prefetches" -n src | sed -n '1,200p'

Last thought: the quick rollback to prefetch=80 is the right immediate mitigation for production. But long-term we need either safer defaults + a guardrail that watchdogs memcpy stalls or architecture changes to avoid prefetch copy contention.

If I forget specifics: start with ramp_traffic + prefetch=1200 and look for 'evict' + 'memcpy_h2d blocked' in logs. Those two together are the smoking gun.

End of scratchpad.

source.title: P0 Postmortem (working draft): EU region GPU capacity exhaustion
source.body:
Working draft that became the source for the final writeup.

Incident date: 2026-02-14
Severity: P0

What happened
- EU (eu-central) ran out of schedulable GPUs in the hosted pool. Router kept accepting traffic and queued requests; tail latencies blew up and then 503s increased.

Impact
- Hosted API in EU: p99 > 25s for ~23 minutes.
- Some customers saw 503s; failover to us-east did not trigger for all tenants because of a policy exception.

Root cause
- Capacity planner thresholds were tuned for average, not burst. A batch of long-context workloads arrived after a model launch email.
- Autoscaler max was capped too low for eu-central due to a stale quota assumption.

Follow-up action items (count these)
1) Raise eu-central autoscaler max and keep a 15% warm buffer for long-context models. Owner team: Eng SRE.
2) Add routing policy that forces cross-region failover when queue depth > threshold for 3 minutes (unless tenant explicitly opted out). Owner team: Eng Platform.
3) Add capacity alert based on schedulable GPUs, not allocated GPUs. Owner team: Eng SRE.
4) Update capacity planning sheet to incorporate launch-day spikes and long-context token distributions. Owner team: Eng SRE.
5) Review and remove the EU failover policy exception created for Tenant-Delta (no longer needed). Owner team: Eng Platform.

Notes
- This doc uses 'Owner team' rather than 'Owning team' but should count the same.

source.title: Rollback loop (RRB-17)  Timeline + internal comms draft (Private upgrades)
source.body:
Working doc  not final

Goal
- Provide a quick internal timeline + a consistent set of phrases for customer comms around the rollback loop / stuck rollback issue in Redwood Private upgrades (RRB-17).

Preferred terminology
- Use: upgrade rollback stuck or rollback loop
- Avoid: infinite loop (sounds scary), data loss (not observed)

Timeline (PT)
- Feb 11: Eng sees repeated rollback retries in lab; workaround identified (delete rollback lock CM + restart controller).
- Feb 12: Customer reports (Acme, Northwind). Incident opened (INC-2147). INT-330 created for patch tracking.
- Feb 13: Root cause confirmed (lock not cleared when cleanup hook exits). PR drafted for fix + loop detection.
- Feb 14: Customer escalation call; validated workaround sequencing + guardrails.
- Feb 16: Draft customer-safe guidance circulated for review.

Customer-safe workaround text (draft)
We have identified a condition where the upgrade rollback can remain in progress longer than expected. A safe remediation is to remove the rollback lock and restart the installer controller. Please confirm you are operating in the `redwood-private` namespace before running the commands.

Commands
1) `kubectl -n redwood-private delete cm installer-rollback-lock`
2) `kubectl -n redwood-private rollout restart deploy/installer-controller`

Notes
- Ask customer to send the installer-controller log segment before and after remediation.
- If RBAC prevents deletion, coordinate with their cluster admin.

Open questions
- Do we want to recommend a read-only confirmation command first? e.g., `kubectl -n redwood-private get cm installer-rollback-lock -o yaml`
- Patch vehicle: 2.9.1 vs 2.9.0 hotfix?

source.title: KV eviction stochastic simulator results (WIP)
source.content:
Purpose and quick summary:

This doc captures the results from a small stochastic simulator I (Jordan) put together to explore how randomized eviction decisions interact with real-world reuse patterns and memory bandwidth peaks. The goal was not to build the final policy, but to get intuition about: 1) how conservative vs aggressive eviction thresholds affect long tail latency and paging, 2) what eviction noise (randomized kicks) does to reuse curves, and 3) how these behaviors map to expected PCIe/GPU memory bandwidth bursts.

Context and motivation
- We've been seeing intermittent latency spikes on <seqlen>1000</seqlen> workloads when KV occupancy approaches 85-95% (see JIRA-RT-1187). On those spikes we observe heavy DMA bursts followed by elevated page faults. Hard to reproduce in lab because of multi-tenant noise.
- Hypothesis: slightly suboptimal eviction heuristics can cause eviction storms (cascade): a few misses cause broad spills which increase bandwidth and cause other pages to be evicted asynchronously, increasing misses.
- Instead of only instrumenting production traces, I wanted a light-weight model to stress different policy knobs and quantify the probability of a cascade under different arrival/reuse distributions.

Simulator overview (quick)
- Discrete-event, single-host model. Simulates a KV cache of N slots (configurable) and a workload generator that emits request sequences with configurable locality/reuse characteristics.
- Key parameters: cache_size_slots, reuse_distribution (zipf/poisson/bimodal), sequence_length, active_clients, eviction_policy (LRU, LFU, recency-weighted with jitter), eviction_random_fraction (0..0.2), eviction_batch_size (1..128), paging_latency_ms (simulated), dma_bandwidth_mb_s (simulated).
- Important simplification: pages are either resident or not; we approximate page migration latency as single constant per configured bandwidth consumption, then allow overlapping DMA up to bandwidth cap.

Workloads used in this run
- short-chatty: 1k distinct keys, Zipf theta=0.9, bursts of 4 requests per user, target qps=6000, seq len 256 tokens (for KV use length)

- multi-tenant-mix: 3 tenants mixed, tenant A heavy cache-local (Zipf0.8), B medium, C tail-heavy (uniform)

Experiment matrix (representative)
- cache sizes: 20k, 50k, 100k slots
- eviction_fraction: 0.0 (deterministic), 0.02, 0.1
- batch_size: 1, 8, 32
- eviction_policy: LRU, recency-weighted (age-decay), frequency-stable(LFU-ish)
- bandwidth caps: 80 GB/s (ideal), 24 GB/s (PCIe gen4 limit emulation), 12 GB/s (constrained)

- steady-state reuse rate (fraction of requests hitting resident KV)
- miss burst probability (probability that within a 500 ms window misses > 5% of requests)

- added latency per request (ms), crude model combining page-in latency and queueing

- We should replay a small sample of production traces with the simulator (trace-to-sim translator) to validate calibration. Marco offered to extract a 10-minute slice from the surge window in PROD and scrub PII. (See linked JIRA).

- Jordan: convert this WIP sim into a small library so it can be parameterized and checked in (RFC in next week). Target: add a CLI to replay traces. (todo: PR skeleton)

- Marco: prototype an adaptive-batch heuristic in simulator and compare to static batch lines.
- Lena: instrument host-level DMA counters in the lab cluster to get better bandwidth curves for calibration.

- Quick hack: seeded LRU with deterministic per-key salt to simulate per-tenant soft limits — this gave lower tenant interferance but is not robust with tenant churn.

- This is not a replacement for production profiling; it is a lightweight tool to reason about combinatorics. Probabilities from sim should not be used directly as SLO estimates.
- We simplified latency model; tail latency contributions from queuing and lock contention are not modeled.

- cache_size_slots: 50000
- workload: short-chatty (zipf theta=0.9)
- active_clients: 120
- eviction_policy: LRU
- eviction_fraction: 0.02
- eviction_batch_size: 8
- paging_latency_ms: 6.0
- dma_bandwidth_mb_s: 24000

If anyone wants the raw CSV outputs or quick plots (time-series + CDFs), ping me and I will drop them into the PR. This is a WIP — want to get some design-eyeballs before filing RFC.

-- Jordan

source.title: Caffeine-powered Bisect Playbook for Quick Regression Triage
source.content:
Context:
- Late-stage dev: small perf/regression spike observed in hosted text-generation v2 route (low-prompt-length).
- Customer report + internal smoke tests: occasional incoherent completions (~0.6% -> 1.9% of sample). Need fast triage before escalation.

Objective (5–20 minute prioritization):
- Rapidly determine if regression is model, routing, or pre/post-processing.
- Produce small reproducible sample set + ad-hoc rubric to categorize failure mode.

Quick hypothesis list (ordered cheap->expensive):
1) Prompt tokenization change (tokenizer update or subtle encoding mismatch).
2) Quantization/precision path change (new kernel selection for short sequences).
3) Routing fallback to smaller model variant under burst.
4) Prompt truncation or prefix drop due to cache bug.
5) Data corruption in the request body at edge (rare but check).

Ad-hoc rubric (for quick labeling):
- Category A: Coherence collapse (output loses sentence-level meaning; hallucinations unrelated to input).
- Category B: Truncation / abrupt cutoff (ends mid-sentence, missing closing).
- Category C: Repetition loop (same phrase/token repeats).
- Category D: Safety / unexpected refusal (sudden policy refusal where none expected).
- Category E: Tokenization artifact (weird tokens/escape sequences present).
- Category F: OK (acceptable).

Sampling plan (cheap, reproducible):
- Pull 120 random requests from the last 48h where route == /v2/generate and token_count < 40. Use existing logging-sampler query: samples:recent-short-prompts-48h.
- Augment with 20 customer-reported examples (from ENGINF-4821).
- Run local reproduce harness (use --deterministic-seed) across three model variants: model-v2.3-fp16, model-v2.3-q4, model-v2.2-fp16.

Checklist for each sample (2–3 minute per batch of 10):
- Reproduce output locally from same model binary + same seed. (Y/N)
- If reproduces: inspect tokens at boundaries for truncation or repeated patterns.
- If not reproducing: check routing logs (timestamp alignment, region, fallback flag), edge proxies for body transforms.
- Record rubric label and short note.

Working examples (kept terse):
- prompt: "Summarize the following paragraph: The experiment showed a 2x improvement..."
  - production output: "It shows 2x improvement ..." -> OK
- prompt: "Write a short blurb about ecosystem services in urban planning."
  - production output: "<s> \u0000 \u0000 0xFF" -> Category E (tokenization artifact)
- prompt: "Explain how to reset a router."
  - production output: "I can't help with that." -> Category D (unexpected refusal)
- prompt: "Give 3 bullets about benefit of caching"
  - production output: repeats "Benefit: reduces cost" 6x -> Category C

Preliminary observations after first 60 samples:
- 8 samples labeled Category E (tokenization artifacts). All originate from same customer region (eu-west-1).
- 4 Category C (repetition) and 1 Category B (truncation).
- Reproduced tokenization artifacts locally when swapping to the q4 kernel path; fp16 path didn't show null bytes.

Immediate hypothesis refinement:
- Kernel selector may be misclassifying some short requests to q4 path that has a packaging bug causing stray null bytes in decode buffer for specific hardware. Explains region skew (different GPU SKU in EU pool).

Quick bisect steps to validate (fast):
1) Re-run 20 failing samples forcing model binary + kernel path to fp16 (bypass q4 route) in staging. If artifact disappears -> kernel path bug.
2) Reproduce one artifact with instrumentation to dump pre-decode bytes and tokenizer output. Compare to healthy run.
3) Check recent release notes: PR 2567 touched kernel selection heuristics for seq_len < 48.
4) If confirmed, create hotfix: pin short-requests to fp16 routing until kernel bug fixed.

Notes for communication to SRE/infra:
- Include sample ids, timestamps, region, and exact node ids where artifacts observed.
- Request temporary routing rule: region eu-west-1 short-requests -> force fp16. Include safe rollback.

Edge checks (non-blocking but important):
- Proxy / edge: Are any intermediate proxies altering Content-Encoding or injecting headers? Quick grep of edge logs shows no gzip anomaly.
- Tokenizer version mismatch: check wheel version pinned in image: tokenizer==1.14.2 both sides.

Next actions (today):
- [ ] Run forced-fp16 reproduce on 20 artifacts (Ravi)
- [ ] Add instrumentation dump for pre-decode bytes and tokenizer outputs (me)
- [ ] Draft temporary routing rule + rollback plan (Aisha)
- [ ] Open follow-up bug with kernel vendor team if confirmed (me)

Open questions / things to watch:
- Why do only some short requests route to q4? (load-based heuristic? weight pruning?)
- Are there any recent infra config flips around kernel selection? Look for terraform changes + recent deploys.
- Monitor rate of Category E—if it rises quickly, escalate to SEV.

Random notes to self (messy):
- Idea: add short-request feature flag to toggle kernel path per-customer for targeted mitigation.
- Consider adding lightweight token-level checksum in debug header for quick detection of corrupt payloads.

Bottom line (for initial sync):
- Early signs point to kernel selection -> q4 path causing occasional decode artifacts on a particular hardware pool. Fast mitigation: force fp16 for short prompts in affected region while we root-cause.

Timestamped audit trail: 2025-02-14 10:02Z - first 60-sample pass done.

-- J.B.

4) Prompt truncation or prefix drop due to cache bug.

- Category A: Coherence collapse (output loses sentence-level meaning; hallucinations unrelated to input).
- Category B: Truncation / abrupt cutoff (ends mid-sentence, missing closing).
- Category C: Repetition loop (same phrase/token repeats).
- Category D: Safety / unexpected refusal (sudden policy refusal where none expected).
- Category E: Tokenization artifact (weird tokens/escape sequences present).

source.title: Meta Instruction Orchestrator - seam proto jotter
source.content:
Context and motivation:
- Quick working notes for an "meta" instruction orchestrator (MIO): an adapter layer that accepts high-level, possibly multi-turn directives and composes them into executable sub-instructions (prompts, tool calls, model variant routing).
- Goal: lightweight runtime shim that sits between client SDK and core runtime; aims to encode composition rules, safety boundaries, and observability hooks at the instruction orchestration level rather than inside each app.
- Target use-cases: multi-step transforms (summarize+translate+extract), user-facing automation flows (generate code + run tests), and tenant-level policies (cost budgets, model pinning).

Non-goals (for this proto):
- Not trying to be a full workflow engine (no long-running state machines).
- Not implementing secure plugin sandboxing here — will assume tool calls are proxied through existing plugin runtime.

High-level responsibilities (MIO):
- Accept a "meta instruction" (structured JSON) from client
- Decompose into instruction graph (linear or DAG) with annotated cost/latency budgets
- Select model variants per node using policy hooks (latency vs cost vs quality)
- Emit execution plan with telemetry hooks and trace ids
- Provide optional per-tenant overrides and quick simulation mode (dry-run)

API shape (draft):
POST /v1/meta-instructions/execute\Request JSON shape (compact):
{
  "request_id": "str",
  "tenant_id": "str",
  "instruction": {
    "kind": "meta/compose|meta/automation|meta/extract",
    "payload": { /* semi-structured; opaque to MIO for now */ }
  },
  "policy": { "budget": { "tokens": 1000, "cost_usd": 0.50 }, "latency_ms": 800 },
  "dry_run": false,
  "trace_context": { "parent_span": "..." }
}

Response (synchronous plan handle):
{
  "plan_id": "str",
  "estimated_cost": "float",
  "estimated_latency_ms": "int",
  "nodes": [ { "node_id":"n1", "type":"llm|tool", "model_hint":"gpt-x|open-llama-q4", "expected_tokens": 120 } ],
  "execution_mode": "sync|async|stream",
  "trace_id": "str"
}

Execution semantics notes:
- sync: MIO will synchronously orchestrate and return final result within 2x latency SLO of the slowest LLM node (only for small graphs).
- async: returns plan_id quickly; client polls GET /v1/meta-instructions/{plan_id}/status or subscribes to callbacks.
- stream: MIO may stream aggregated partial outputs as nodes complete (useful for progressive UX).

Config schema (tenant policy / example):
{
  "tenant_id": "acme",
  "defaults": {
    "model_priority": ["redwood-aviary-small","open-llama-7b-q4"],
    "max_tokens_per_request": 2048,
    "cost_safeguard": { "max_per_call_usd": 1.00 }
  },
  "overrides": {
    "sensitive": { "require_safety_review": true }
  }
}

Adaptive routing policy sketch (DSL-y):
- if latency_ms_budget < 400 then prefer model variant with latency <= 150ms and quality_score >= 0.6
- if instruction.kind == "meta/automation" and tenant.role == "beta" then allow experimental-models:true
- repr: simple JSON rules evaluated by MIO policy engine; rule engine should be pluggable (lua/rego e.g.).

Telemetry/observability (minimum):
- events: instruction.received, plan.computed, node.dispatched, node.completed, plan.completed, plan.failed
- payload: plan_id, request_id, tenant_id, node_id, model_variant, tokens_used, cost_estimate, cost_actual, latency_ms, error_code
- traces: span per node + parent span for the plan; include sampling hint (important for heavy flows)
- logs: structured JSON lines for plan lifecycle (kept in console and shipped to long-term traces in dedicated plan logs)

Sequence diagram (ASCII rough):
Client -> MIO: POST /execute (meta instruction)
MIO -> Policy: eval(tenant, instruction) -> ruleset
MIO -> Planner: decompose(instruction) -> graph
Planner -> ModelRouter: choose variants per node
MIO -> Runtime: dispatch node1 -> model A -> result1
Runtime -> MIO: node1 complete
MIO -> Runtime: dispatch node2 (tool) -> tool -> result2
Runtime -> MIO: node2 complete
MIO -> Client: final aggregated result (or partial streams as each node completes)

Edge cases and failure modes:
- model overload: automatic fallback to next variant in priority list; mark plan with degraded_quality flag
- tool failure: if node is optional, continue; if required, mark plan failed and return detailed node error + remediation suggestion
- cross-tenant quota exhaustion: short-circuit with a user-facing retry-after + plan-level credit reservation attempt

Security notes:
- MIO should never store raw PII for longer than retention policy; pass-through with masking rule for telemetry (hashing/partial redaction)
- policy evaluation must run in a sandbox (no dynamic code from tenant)

Quick implementation sketch (modules):
- API layer (Flask/FastAPI) -> validate/add trace -> enqueue to Planner / or synchronous planner
- Planner (in-memory graph builder for small graphs, queue fallback for large)
- Policy engine (rego or in-house rules) -> returns routing decision + safety flags
- ModelRouter -> consults model catalog + tenant config
- Dispatcher -> talks to existing runtime endpoints (hosted/dedicated/private) via unified runtime client
- Observability shim -> emits events+spans via existing telemetry collector (otel)

Developer UX experiments to try:
- small REPL UI: paste a meta instruction and get visual graph; allow drag/drop to change node ordering and re-estimate cost
- dry-run mode that returns plan without touching runtime (useful for product demos and cost previews)

Open questions / TODOs (prioritized):
- Q1: How expressive should the instruction payload be? freeform text vs structured ops? (TODO: prototype hybrid where top-level keys are ops + freeform prompt fields)
- Q2: What are safe defaults for model priority per tenant? (TODO: sample production telemetry and compute histogram of latencies/costs by model)
- Q3: Retry semantics for streaming plans (idempotence markers across node retries)
- Q4: Where to persist plan metadata? short-lived in MIO cache vs persistent for audit? (opt: write completed plans to long-term store if cost>threshold)
- Implement minimal integration tests: unit for planner, e2e dry-run, policy sandbox fuzzing

Notes-to-self:
- keep the API minimalish: start with execute(dry_run=true) and execute(async) paths
- reach out to Lina for policy-rule language preferences (rego vs lua)
- draft a quick console wireframe to show estimated cost + node map

References / inspiration:
- ticket JIRA-ENG-885 (multi-step-tooling workstream)
- confluence://INFER-arch/meta-instruction-orch (proto notes)
- similar patterns observed in product: "compose" middleware in agents experiments (we have some earlier half-baked libs)

End of jotter.

(untidy, intentionally — will prune after first spike)
